current_uses_and_zoning.describe_the_cur.answer:
The property is currently improved with a one-story masonry commercial building constructed in approximately 1974, totaling about 4,200 square feet, situated on a roughly 0.32-acre lot with on-site surface parking. For the past several years, the building has been used as a small neighborhood retail center with a mix of tenants. At the time of this request, the building is configured into three tenant spaces, of which two are occupied and one is vacant.

The easternmost suite (Suite A, approximately 1,800 square feet) is occupied by a convenience retail tenant operating under the name "32nd Street Market." This tenant sells packaged foods, beverages, toiletries, over-the-counter medicines, and household essentials. There is no fuel sales, no alcohol consumption on-site, and no commercial kitchen; only pre-packaged and refrigerated items are sold. Hours of operation are generally 8:00 AM to 9:00 PM, seven days a week. Deliveries are made by box truck and small delivery vans and occur primarily at the rear of the building using the existing loading area accessed from the alley.

The middle suite (Suite B, approximately 1,400 square feet) is currently vacant. Historically, this space has been used as a small office and service space—most recently it housed a tax preparation and notary service. The interior is still configured with a small waiting area, two private offices, a storage room, and a single restroom. There are no industrial or high-intensity uses in this space, and no hazardous materials are stored on-site.

The western suite (Suite C, approximately 1,000 square feet) is occupied by a neighborhood hair salon and barbershop called "Westside Cuts & Styles." This business provides haircuts, styling, and basic grooming services. The salon typically operates from 9:00 AM to 7:00 PM Tuesday through Saturday, with occasional extended hours during prom and holiday seasons. The use is low-impact and generates limited noise, with most activity limited to customer arrivals and departures and normal conversation inside the building.

The site includes a paved parking lot in front of the building with approximately 14 striped parking spaces, including one accessible space near the primary entrance. There is a narrow landscaped strip between the sidewalk and the parking area with grass and a few small ornamental trees. Trash and recycling are stored in two commercial dumpsters located in a fenced enclosure at the rear of the property, accessed from the alley. Exterior lighting is limited to wall-mounted fixtures on the building and a small pole light in the parking lot, all of which are downcast to minimize light spillover onto neighboring residential properties.

Surrounding uses are primarily single-family residential to the north and west, with a mix of residential and institutional uses to the south and east. The existing commercial building has historically functioned as a neighborhood-serving retail node, providing essential goods and personal services within walking distance for nearby residents. There are no outdoor sales, no outdoor storage, and no drive-through facilities on the site. Signage consists of individual tenant wall signs on the building façade and a low monument sign near the front property line.

Overall, the property is being used in a manner consistent with a small, neighborhood-scale commercial center: low-intensity retail and personal service uses that primarily serve the immediate area, with limited traffic generation and minimal off-site impacts.
